q: Asteroids, meteors and comets are _________ satellites.
feedback: Asteroids, meteors and comets are natural satellites. Satellites that are not man made and exist naturally are called Natural satellites. On the other hand satellites that are  manmade are called Artificial satellites.

subject: Science
q: _ asteroid is larger than the _ asteroid.
feedback: Ceres is the largest asteroid that exists in space with a diameter of 933km.Vista is half the size of Ceres.

subject: Science

feedback: Located between mars and jupiter is a band of asteroids known as asteroid belt.

feedback: Asteroids are composed of minerals,metals and rocks.Whereas comets are composed of dust and snow.

subject: Science
q: There is a lonely rock going between Mars and Jupiter. What is it called?
feedback: The lonely rock is called meteoroid. Where asteroids are always found in the form of a band between mars and jupiter, we can also find some meteoroids roaming alone in this region.

subject: Science
q: Why does a meteor catch fire when it is entering the Earth?
options: high speed leads to friction, and friction creates heat
meteroid is composed of materials that catch fire easily
feedback: High speed leads to friction, and friction creates heat. A meteor moving through the vacuum of space typically travels at speeds reaching tens of thousands of miles per hour. When the meteor hits the atmosphere, the air in front of it compresses incredibly quickly. When a gas is compressed, its temperature rises.In other words it heats up due to friction from the air.  This causes the meteor to heat up so much that it glows.

subject: Science
q: Comet can only be seen when it is very close to _.
feedback: Comets can only be seen when they are very close to sun. The long tail of comet is formed from gases due to heat of sun. We can see a number of comets with the naked eye when they pass close to the sun because their comas and tails reflect sunlight or even glow because of energy they absorb from the sun.

subject: Science
q: Shooting stars are also called _.
feedback: Meteors are also called shooting stars.The sand-sized grains of dust sometimes slam into the thin air that surrounds the Earth, called the atmosphere, where they get hot very quickly. As the dust grains burn up, they leave bright streaks of light across the night sky. These streaks are called meteors, often nicknamed 'shooting stars' or 'falling stars'.

subject: Science
q: When a meteorite falls on the Earth's surface it creates a __________.
feedback: When meteorites fall on the Earth's surface they form large holes called craters.

feedback: Single celled organisms are called unicellular organisms. Meaning, they are made up of just one cell. Organisms made up of more than one cell are known as multicellular organisms.

feedback: Cells are basic units of all the living things. Organisation of a life follows as :|Cells-> tissues->organs->organ systems->organisms

subject: Science
q: Who discovered cells?
feedback: The cell was first discovered and named by Robert Hooke in 1665. He remarked that it looked strangely similar to the cellular or small rooms which monks inhabited, thus deriving the name, Cells!

subject: Science
q: If something is very small we use _ to see it.
feedback: Microscope is an optical instrument used to see very small objects. It uses lenses in order to magnify the objects so that they can be seen through the naked eye.

subject: Science
q: When someone gets injured, ________ cells become active and help to fight the germs.
feedback: White blood cells are our fighting champs. They defend our body against bacterias and viruses and prevent us from diseases. They are always at war against the foreign invaders. When our body is in under attack by germs, white blood cells flow through the bloodstream. They rush in to help destroy the harmful substance and prevent illness.

subject: Science
q: You saw a small organism moving on your table. What is it?
feedback: It is a multicellular organism. Unicellular organisms are very tiny. They can only be seen with the help of a microscope. Therefore anything that can be seen with the naked eye can not be unicellular.

subject: Science
q: Brain is made up of _______ cells.
feedback: Brain is composed of billions and trillions of nerve cells. Blood is composed of red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets. Thus the only option left is nerve cells.

subject: Science
q: Many cells join together to form ____________.
feedback: Cells group together to form tissues.

subject: Science
q: Which parts of a plant cell are only visible after proper staining and viewing under a high-power lens of a microscope?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: We can view different parts of a cell through a light microscope. These include the cell wall, vacuoles, cytoplasm, chloroplast, nucleus, and the cell membrane. A light microscope uses lenses and light to magnify cell parts. However, it can usually achieve a maximum magnification of 2000×, which is not sufficient to see many other tiny organelles like mitochondria and ribosomes.

subject: Science
q: Which pair of statements is correct for a plant cell?
options: the outer boundary is the cell membrane; the sap vacuole is outside the cytoplasm
the outer boundary is the cell membrane; the sap vacuole is surrounded by the cytoplasm
the outer boundary is the cell wall; the sap vacuole is outside the cytoplasm
the outer boundary is the cell wall; the sap vacuole is surrounded by the cytoplasm
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: In a plant cell, the outermost boundary is the cell wall. The liquid portion of a cell is called the cytoplasm. It holds many structures, including a large central sap vacuole.

subject: Science
q: Which features do animal cells share with plant cells?
options: both cells have chloroplasts and cytoplasm
both cells have a cell wall and a nucleus
both cells have cytoplasm and a nucleus
both cells have cell membranes and centrioles
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: The structures that are common between a plant and an animal cell are the cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, ribosomes, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, and Golgi bodies.

subject: Science
q: The diagram shows an animal cell. Which part is partially permeable?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: A cell membrane is partially permeable. An animal cell is surrounded by a cell membrane. It allows selective substances to move across it.

subject: Science
q: Which of the labelled features of an animal cell are absent in a plant cell?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: An animal cell has centrioles, whereas, a plant cell does not have any centrioles. All other organelles, vacuoles, mitochondria, and nuclei are present in both animal and plant cells.

subject: Science
q: Which statement is correct about cell walls?
options: It is present in an animal and a plant cell.
It is absent in an animal cell, but present outside the cell membrane in a plant cell.
It is present in an animal cell, but absent in a plant cell.
It is present outside the cell membrane in an animal cell, but absent in a plant cell.
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: The cell wall is the outermost boundary of a plant cell and surrounds the selective permeable cell membrane. It is absent in an animal cell.

subject: Science
q: Which structure inside the nucleus controls the production of ribosomes?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: Chromosomes are threadlike structures present inside the nucleus. They contain genes which control all cell activities, including the production of ribosomes.

subject: Science
q: A student prepared a slide of cells from a given sample and observed it under a microscope. The image formed in the microscope is given below. How can he identify the cells as plant or animal cells?
options: He realises that these are animal cells because they have a cytoplasm and a cell membrane.
He realises that these are animal cells because they have a nucleus.
He realises that these are plant cells because they have a cytoplasm and a cell membrane.
He realises that these are plant cells because they have a cell wall, a big central vacuole, and a nucleus.
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: The image shows plant cells because the cell wall is the outermost boundary. The second identifying factor is the big central vacuole that occupies most of the space inside the cell, pushing the nucleus to the side. Animal cells have a central nucleus, a cytoplasm, and a cell membrane. However, they do not have cell walls, and have more than one vacuole.

subject: Science
q: Which structure inside the nucleus controls the production of ribosomes?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: Chromosomes are threadlike structures present inside the nucleus. They contain genes which control all cellular activities, including the production of ribosomes.

subject: Science
q: The following diagrams show two cells. Which of these cells contains vacuoles?
options: Y in cell 1 and Z in cell 2
X in cell 1 and Y in cell 2
Y in cell 1 and X in cell 2
Z in cell 1 and Z in cell 2
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: Cell 1 is a plant cell and cell 2 is an animal cell. Both cells have vacuoles. However, the plant cell only has one large vacuole labelled as Y, while the animal cell has many vacuoles labelled as X as seen in the given images.

subject: Science
q: The diagram shows some animal cells as they appear under a microscope. How many cell walls can be seen?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: In the given diagram, the total number of cell walls present is 0 because animal cells do not have cell walls. The outermost boundary of an animal cell is the cell membrane.

subject: Science
q: The diagram shows a plant cell. Identify the labelled parts of the plant cell.
options: 1. cell membrane; 2. cell wall; 3. chloroplast; 4. cytoplasm
1. cell membrane; 2. cell wall; 3. cytoplasm; 4. chloroplast
1. cell wall; 2. cell membrane; 3. cytoplasm; 4. chloroplast
1. cell wall; 2. cell membrane; 3. chloroplast; 4. cytoplasm
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: The cell wall (2) is the outermost boundary of a plant cell which surrounds the selective permeable cell membrane (1). The cell membrane encloses the cytoplasm (4), which is the fluid part of the cell. A plant cell has many oval-shaped structures floating in the cytoplasm. These structures are called chloroplasts (3).

subject: Science
q: The diagram shows a plant cell.  Which part of the cell absorbs light?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: Label II is chloroplast. Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll which absorbs light energy to carry out the process of photosynthesis. Label I is a vacuole, and label III is a nucleus.

subject: Science
q: Which part of the cell produces energy by breaking down glucose?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: Mitochondria are sausage-shaped structures that are considered the power generators of the cell. They do this by breaking down glucose and providing energy for cell activities.

subject: Science
q: Which part of the plant cell controls what enters or leaves the cell?
skill: Differentiate between an animal cell and a plant cell
feedback: The cell membrane, also known as the plasma membrane, surrounds the cell and separates the cytoplasm (the contents of the cell) from its surrounding environment. It is selectively permeable and controls what enters or leaves the cell. The cell wall gives structure to the cell.

subject: Science
q: The smallest units of life in plants are ____.
feedback: Cells are the smallest units of all living things plants and animals.

feedback: Plant has two major organ system.

q: Which plant organ makes food?
feedback: Food is made in the leaf of a plant by the process of photosynthesis.

subject: Science
q: Which of these is NOT a plant organ?
feedback: Stomata is the opening between two guard cells present  in the leaf of a plant.

subject: Science
q: Which of these is NOT a function of roots?
feedback: Seeds are produced in the flowers of a plant. Roots absorbs water and minerals from soil and provide support to the plant.

subject: Science
q: Removal of which of the following two systems will cause the plant to die?
feedback: A plant can grow back from the root even if the shoot system is removed. However, if the root system is removed the plant won't survive as it won't get any minerals and water necessary for photosynthesis and also because there will be no support for the shoot system to stand by.

Xylem → Vascular bundle → Leaf → Shoot system.
Which statement is false about the given organisation?
options: xylem is a cell found in vascular bundle
vascular bundle is found in xylem tissue
leaf is an organ of shoot system
flower is an organ of  shoot system
feedback: According to the cellular orgazisation in plants |Cells → Tissues → Organs →Organ systems.|  Xylem is a cell found in vascular bundle which is a tissue found in the organ leaf, which is part of the Shoot system. Xylem is not a tissue it is a cell therefore the second option is false.

subject: Science
q: Vascular bundles are usually present in the _ of the plant.
feedback: Vascular bundles are usually present in the stem of the plant because they help in transferring water and nutrients up and down the plant.

q: Our bodies lose water in the processes of _____ in the form of vapours and sweat.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: Our bodies lose water during respiration in the form of water vapour and during excretion in the form of sweat and urine.

subject: Science
q:
What are possible consequences of obesity?
I.   heart diseases
II.  high blood pressure
III. diabetes
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: Obesity, or continuous overeating, results in diseases like heart attack, blood pressure, and diabetes.

subject: Science
q: Why do we need to keep our bodies hydrated?
options: Drinking water keeps us full for longer and thus we do not gain excess weight.
The human body needs water to perform most of its important functions.
Drinking water helps a person gain weight if he/she is underweight.
Drinking water is important so that our bodies can retain excess fluid.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
We should drink plenty of water and keep our bodies hydrated, because water helps our bodies perform most of the important functions, like circulation, digestion, and in absorption and transportation of nutrients.

We cannot lose weight or gain weight just by drinking water. To lose or gain weight, we need to watch our food intake and pair it with some exercises.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following are advantages of exercising?

I.   increase in physical strength
II.  weight control
III. better mental health
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: There are numerous advantages of exercising. Exercise increases our physical strength and helps in weight control. It also improves mental health by reducing anxiety and depression and by improving self-esteem.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about mental health and is TRUE?
options: It doesn't really matter whether one sleeps for four hours or eight hours.
There is no relationship between sleep and weight gain.
Sleep is closely related to mental health.
Everyone can function well with 3 hours of sleep.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Sleep and mental health are closely connected.

Sleep affects our psychological state and mental health. Those with mental health problems are more likely to have sleep disorders.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following are ways to remain healthy?

I.   Get enough sleep.
II.  Keep oneself hydrated.
III. Exercise regularly.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Getting enough sleep, keeping our bodies hydrated, and exercising regularly are important to live a healthy life.

Getting enough sleep is as important to our bodies as eating, drinking or breathing. It helps in reducing stress, improving mood, and remaining focused. It also boosts our immune system.

subject: Science
q:
Abdullah goes to a gym every day and exercises for almost two hours to keep himself fit and healthy. He also eats beans every day. Despite the hard work, he doesn't feel active. Which of the following statements about Abdullah's condition is/are TRUE?

I.   He should exercise more to stay active.
II.  Eating the same food everyday may have resulted in a deficiency of nutrients in his body.
III. He is overworking at the gym.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Excess of everything can be bad, be it exercise.

Overexercising can negatively impact our bodies. It can lead to stress, hormone imbalance, and exhaustion.

Eating the same food types everyday results in a deficiency of nutrients in one's body. If we continue to consume the same food every day, our bodies will lack several essential nutrients that are not part of that food source.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements is FALSE about physical health?
options: Physically fit people are less likely to fall ill because they have a strong immune system.
There is no relationship between physical and mental health.
Eating the same food type every day may result in lack of essential nutrients in our body.
Exercise is important to stay physically fit and healthy.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
The statement, there is no relationship between physical and mental health is FALSE. A sound body supports a sound mind.

Physical and mental health are interconnected. Poor physical health can lead to mental health problems. Similarly, poor mental health can negatively affect the physical health.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following can be harmful for our health?

I.   consuming alcohol
II.  not drinking water
III. playing mobile games occasionally
IV. eating fries everyday
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Consuming alcohol, not drinking enough water, and eating fries everyday can be harmful for our health.
Fries contain high amounts of fats. A balanced diet does not consist of a large amount of fats.

Playing games on mobile phones occasionally doesn't affect our health.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is a good way to remain active and healthy?
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Walking everyday is an easy and good way to remain active and healthy.

Watching TV or playing videogames is fun, but they don't contribute to our physical health.

Similarly, as per the famous proverb, all work and no play makes Jack a dull boy, continuous studying without rest or relaxation can be harmful for our mental and physical health.

subject: Science
q: _____ shows the different food groups and how much of each food group creates a healthy diet.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: A food pyramid is a chart that shows that how many servings of a food group should be consumed in a healthy and balanced diet. The food at the bottom should be consumed more than the food at the top as indicated by the sizes of their sections.

subject: Science
q: What would happen if a person doesn't have a balanced diet?
options: There will be no effect on his/her physical or mental health.
His/her eyesight will become weak within a week.
He/she will have a strong immune system due to lack of nutrients.
He/she will be more likely to suffer from a physical disorder or malnutrition.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
It is necessary to eat a variety of foods in the right proportion. A balanced diet supplies the nutrients that our bodies need to function effectively.

If a person doesn't consume a balanced diet, he/she is more likely to suffer from a physical disorder due to a lack of nutrients.

subject: Science
q: Why should we exercise?
options: to make ourselves hungry all the time
to spend less time on studies
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: We should exercise to stay fit and make our bodies strong. Exercise increases stamina and endurance, both of which make our bodies strong.

subject: Science
q: It is important to consume a balanced diet so that
options: we are able to eat everything when we go on an expedition.
our bodies are prone to diseases, infections, and fatigue.
we are not scolded by our parents on not eating vegetables.
we get all the necessary nutrients for our body to function properly.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: It is important to consume a balanced diet so that we get all the necessary nutrients for our body to function properly. Our bodies need a variety of minerals and vitamins to function and this variety can be attained by having a balanced diet.

subject: Science
q: Why is it important to stay active?
options: to always stand first in class
to be able to fight with neighbours
to improve health and reduce the risk of falling ill
to be able to stay awake for more than 24 hours
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: We all need to stay fit and healthy because a healthy person is less likely to fall ill compared to an unhealthy fellow. Staying active is a very important way to generally stay in good health.

subject: Science
q: The diagram shows some vegetables. What substance in the vegetables help bowel movements occur?
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback:
Fibre in vegetables adds bulk to stool, helping bowel movements and, therefore, the stool to pass more quickly through the intestines.
Foods high in fibre include wheat bran, whole grains, cereals, seeds, legumes, and many fruits and vegetables. Vegetables like potatoes, beet, broccoli, and carrots are fibre-rich.

subject: Science
q: The diagram shows the digestive system. Identify the part of the gut that will be most affected by constipation.
options: both III and IV will be affected
both I and II will be affected
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback:
Undigested food is turned into faeces in the large intestine. During constipation, the stool moves slowly along the large intestine. The prolonged stay of stool causes more water to be extracted from the stool and reabsorbed in the intestine walls. This makes the stool hard and dry.
The hardened stools are stored in the rectum (II) temporarily, causing adverse effects there such as bacterial growth and laceration of intestinal walls.

subject: Science
q: The condition in which bowel movements are too frequent and watery is
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Diarrhoea is a condition in which faeces are discharged from the bowels frequently and in a liquid form. Diarrhoea is associated with abdominal pain (cramps), nausea, and bloating (swelling).

subject: Science
q: The disease with difficult bowel movements is called _____
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Constipation is the condition in which a person finds it difficult to empty the bowels, usually associated with hardened faeces. Fibre-rich food adds bulk to stool, helping the stool pass more quickly through the intestines.

subject: Science
q: Antiperistalsis may lead to
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback:
Antiperistalsis is reverse peristaltic action in which the contents of the intestines move backwards, resulting in vomiting.
Peristalsis is the muscular, wave-like movement in the gut that pushes food along the digestive tract.

subject: Science
q: Diarrhoea takes place due to
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Diarrhoea is a condition in which one passes semi-liquid faeces. It may be caused by an infection, eating contaminated food, a reaction to some medicine, or just anxiety or excitement.

subject: Science
q: The entrance of germs into the body through food is known as
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Food poisoning is an illness caused by bacteria or other toxins in food, typically associated with vomiting and diarrhoea.

subject: Science
q: The main cause of indigestion of food is _____.
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Indigestion is pain or discomfort in the stomach associated with difficulty in digesting food. The main cause of indigestion is a lack of chewing. Chewing or mastication is the process by which food is crushed and ground by teeth.

subject: Science
q: In constipation, faeces are _____.
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Constipation is a condition in which a person has difficulty emptying the bowels, usually associated with hard and dry faeces.

subject: Science
q:
The following conditions prevent which type of digestive disorder?

I. maintain proper hygiene
II. wash fruits and vegetables well before eating
III. eat properly cooked meals
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Diarrhoea is a condition in which one passes semi-liquid faeces. It may be caused by an infection, eating contaminated food, a reaction to some medicine, or just anxiety or excitement. Diarrhoea can be prevented if hands are properly washed with soap, fruits and vegetables are properly washed before cooking or eating, and by eating properly cooked food.

subject: Science
q: ______ happen(s) when your body doesn't have as much water as it needs.
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Dehydration occurs when more water and fluids leave the body than enter it. While mild dehydration may be just uncomfortable, severe dehydration can be fatal.

subject: Science
q: Diarrhoea can lead to _____, which can further complicate other bodily functions.
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback:
One of the symptoms of diarrhoea is loose, watery stools at least three or more times a day. During this, a lot of water is lost from the body. A prolonged case of diarrhoea or vomiting can cause the body to lose more fluid than it can take in.
The result is dehydration, which occurs when your body doesn't have the fluid it needs to function properly. While mild dehydration may be just uncomfortable, severe dehydration can be fatal.

subject: Science
q: _____ food passes slowly through the gut, forming sticky _____.
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Low fibre food limits bowel movements. Food movement through the gut slows down, which results in the formation of greasy, pale, and sticky lumps.

subject: Science
q: To prevent constipation, we should eat _____ regularly.
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Constipation is a condition in which there is difficulty in emptying the bowels, usually associated with hardened faeces. Fibre-rich food adds bulk to the stool, helping the stool pass more quickly through the intestines.

subject: Science
q: Diarrhoea symptoms include abdominal pain, _____, and _____.
skill: Discuss the common disorders of the digestive system
feedback: Diarrhoea is a condition in which faeces are discharged from the bowels frequently and in a semi-liquid form. Diarrhoea is associated with abdominal pain (cramps), nausea, and bloating (swelling).

q: The energy of motion is called _____ energy.
skill: Define energy and identify different forms of energy with examples
feedback: The energy a body possesses due to its motion is known as kinetic energy. If a body has greater speed, it will have more kinetic energy.

subject: Science
q: When work is done on an object, _____ is transferred to that object.
skill: Define energy and identify different forms of energy with examples
feedback: Energy is the ability of a body to do work. When work is done on an object, energy is transferred to that object. This is because energy cannot be created or destroyed.

subject: Science
q: The ability to do work is the definition of _____
skill: Define energy and identify different forms of energy with examples
feedback: Energy is defined as the ability to do work. Energy is the quantitative property that must be transferred to an object to perform this work.

subject: Science
q: What cannot happen to energy?
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback: Energy can be transferred, changed, or transformed but energy cannot be destroyed.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about energy is FALSE?
options: Energy in a system remains constant.
Energy is conserved when transferred from one body to another.
Whenever energy passes from one body to another, it changes into mechanical energy.
Energy is often dissipated into the atmosphere in form of heat and sound energy.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
Energy is conserved and the total energy in a system remains constant.
Energy is often dissipated into the atmosphere in the form of heat and sound.

Energy does not necessarily change into mechanical energy when passed from one system to another.
It changes into mechanical energy where desired, like in fans, vehicles, washing machines, and electric motors.

subject: Science
q: How is energy conserved when electric bulbs are supplied with electricity?
options: The electrical energy changes into light energy only.
The electrical energy changes into heat energy only.
The electrical energy changes into sound energy.
The electrical energy changes into light and heat energy.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
When a bulb is supplied with electricity, some of the energy changes into light energy and rest is dissipated to the environment in the form of heat.
The total electrical energy is equal to the sum of light energy and dissipated heat energy.
So total energy remains constant.

subject: Science
q: We can observe the law of conservation of energy
options: in an ideal and frictionless environment.
in our classrooms in an environment with friction.
in a swing in a park.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
In an ideal and frictionless environment, the law of conservation of energy can be observed as there will be no energy dissipation due to friction.
A swinging pendulum will keep swinging and a moving ball will keep moving in a frictionless system.
Also, there will be no energy loss.

subject: Science
q: If energy cannot be created or destroyed, then what happens to the kinetic energy of a moving ball after travelling some distance?
options: Its kinetic energy increases as it moves.
Its kinetic energy is dissipated into the air due to friction.
Its kinetic energy changes into chemical energy as it moves.
Its kinetic energy changes into light energy as it moves.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
A moving ball eventually stops after travelling some distance.
This is because the kinetic energy of the ball dissipates into the air in the form of heat, due to air friction and gravitational force.

subject: Science
q: If energy cannot be created or destroyed, then why does energy disappear after some time when supplied to a system?
options: due to dissipation of energy into the atmosphere
due to conversion of heat into the desired form of energy
due to the cold temperature around us
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
When energy is supplied to a system, it decreases or disappears with the passage of time.
This is because energy is lost or dissipated into the environment.

subject: Science
q: How is energy conserved when a ball is thrown up?
options: The kinetic energy of the ball changes into potential energy as the ball moves up.
Kinetic energy does not change and remains the same.
The potential energy of the ball changes into kinetic energy as the ball moves up.
Potential energy does not change and remains the same.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
When we throw up a ball, we provide kinetic energy to it.
The kinetic energy makes the ball move. As the ball moves upward, the kinetic energy of the ball changes into potential energy due to the height.
When the ball reaches its maximum height, all the kinetic energy is changed into potential energy.

subject: Science
q: What will happen if we swing a pendulum with some speed in the absence of air?
options: The pendulum will stop swinging eventually.
The pendulum will swing at a higher speed.
The pendulum will slow down but will not stop.
The pendulum will keep swinging with the same speed.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
In the absence of air, the pendulum will keep swinging with the same speed for an infinite amount of time.
This is because there will be no friction due to air and hence no dissipation of energy takes place.

subject: Science
q: While moving on a swing, we observe that the swing stops for a while when it reaches the top. This is because
options: kinetic energy becomes maximum at that point.
kinetic energy becomes zero at that point.
potential energy becomes zero at that point.
all the kinetic energy changes into heat energy at that point.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
When we are swinging on a swing, the swing stops for a while when it is changing its direction.
This is because all the kinetic energy changes into potential energy at the top and kinetic energy becomes zero.
Due to the absence of kinetic energy, the swing stops for a while.

subject: Science
q: Which form of energy does a pendulum have between the points A and C?
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
At point A, the pendulum only has potential energy, and at point C it has kinetic energy only.
Between point A and C, the pendulum has both kinetic and potential energy.
As the bob of the pendulum starts moving from point A to C, the potential energy changes into kinetic energy and potential energy becomes zero at point C.
On the other hand, kinetic energy changes into potential energy as the bob moves from point C to A.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements BEST describes the law of conservation of energy?
options: Energy is lost in the atmosphere when changed from one form to another.
The Sun is the main source of energy and provides heat and light energy.
The total energy in a system remains constant.
There are different forms of energy.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
The law of conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created nor destroyed.
However, it can be changed from one form to another, but the total energy in the system remains constant.

subject: Science
q: When we rub our hands,
options: the potential energy in our body changes into kinetic energy.
mechanical energy changes into heat energy due to friction.
the chemical energy of our hands changes into potential energy.
heat energy in our body changes into chemical energy.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback:
We get energy to work, walk, and move from the food we eat.
This energy changes into mechanical energy when we move our hands.
The mechanical energy changes into heat energy due to friction when we rub our hands.

subject: Science
q: During the swinging of a pendulum, interconversion between _____ and _____ energies take place.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback: When a pendulum swings, potential energy changes into kinetic energy and vice versa.

subject: Science
q: The law of conservation of energy states that
options: energy can be passed from one body or system to another.
during energy transfer, energy changes into an unwanted form of energy.
energy cannot be created or destroyed, and remains constant.
energy cannot be created, destroyed, or transferred.
skill: Law of conservation of energy
feedback: The law of conservation of energy states that <b>energy cannot be created or destroyed<b/>. It remains constant in a system.

subject: Science
